perturbation.original_text: 6.1 If either party decides not to become, or continue to be involved in the Purpose with the other party it shall notify the other party in writing immediately. The obligations of each party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or discussions between the parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of two (2) years from the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 If either party decides not to become, or continue to be involved in the Purpose with the other party it shall notify the other party in writing immediately. The obligations of each party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or discussions between the parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of ten (10) years from the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Extended the confidentiality obligations to 10 years which may be unreasonably long and thus unenforceable under UK law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5 of the UK Limitation Act 1980 generally sets a limitation period of six years for claims based on simple contract. While confidentiality agreements are not directly addressed, a 10-year period could be challenged if deemed unreasonable or exceeding what's necessary to protect legitimate business interests. This depends on the specific information and industry. The longer the period the more likely it could be challenged under general contractual principles of fairness and reasonableness.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a two-year confidentiality obligation post-termination, while the changed text extends this to ten years. The Law Explanation suggests that a ten-year period may be considered unreasonable under UK law, potentially conflicting with general contractual principles of fairness. The Limitation Act 1980, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, sets a six-year limitation for claims based on simple contract, which, while not directly applicable, establishes a benchmark for reasonableness. Extending the confidentiality period significantly beyond this benchmark introduces a contradiction, as it may be deemed unenforceable or against public policy.

perturbation.original_text: 10.2 Any notice shall be deemed to have been duly received: (a) If delivered personally, when left at the address and for the contact referred to in this clause; or (b) If sent by pre-paid first class post or recorded delivery, at 9.00 am on the second Business Day after posting; or (c) If delivered by commercial courier, on the date and at the time that the courier's delivery receipt is signed.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.2 Any notice shall be deemed to have been duly received: (a) If delivered personally, when left at the address and for the contact referred to in this clause; or (b) If sent by pre-paid first class post or recorded delivery, at 5.00 pm on the fifth Business Day after posting; or (c) If delivered by commercial courier, seven days after the courier's delivery receipt is signed.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the deemed receipt time for notices sent via post or courier to five business days and seven days respectively, creates potential issues under English contract law, specifically regarding timely communication for critical actions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Contract Law - Reasonableness of Notice
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of Contract Law - Reasonableness of Notice
perturbation.law_explanation: While English law doesn't specify exact timeframes for notice, it requires reasonableness. A delay of five business days for postal delivery and seven days for courier delivery may be deemed unreasonable, especially for time-sensitive matters. Courts consider the context and urgency when assessing reasonableness. Setting such extended timelines could invalidate the notice if a quicker method was available and necessary.
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the deemed receipt time for notices, potentially conflicting with the legal requirement of 'reasonableness' in communication, especially for time-sensitive matters. While specific laws about notice periods aren't provided in the snippets, the Law Explanation indicates that extending the timelines could invalidate the notice if a quicker method was available and necessary, based on the principle of 'reasonableness' under English law. The original text implies quicker receipt times; the altered text significantly extends them, which could be deemed unreasonable. This falls under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 3.  RETURN OF INFORMATION 3.1 At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) Destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) Erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information from its computer systems or which is stored in electronic form (to the extent possible); and (c) Certify in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or any applicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipient to keep evidence that it has performed its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.  RETURN OF INFORMATION 3.1 At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) Destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) Erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information from its computer systems or which is stored in electronic form (to the extent possible); and (c) The recipient is not required to certify in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this clause.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for written certification of compliance with the information return obligations undermines the disclosing party's ability to enforce the agreement and contradicts standard practices for demonstrating fulfillment of contractual duties.
perturbation.law_citation: General Principles of Contract Law - Burden of Proof
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a codified law, the principle that a party must be able to provide evidence of compliance with contractual obligations is fundamental. Removing the certification requirement makes it difficult for the Disclosing Party to prove the Recipient fulfilled their obligation to return or destroy confidential information. This creates a disadvantage for the Disclosing Party in any potential dispute, and goes against the principle of evidential certainty in contract performance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: burden of proof | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you burden of proof Generally, burden of proof describes the standard that a party seeking to prove a fact in court must satisfy to have that fact legally established. There are different standards for different circumstances. For example, in criminal cases , the burden of proving the defendant ’s guilt is on the prosecution , and they must establish that fact beyond a reasonable doubt . In civil cases, the plaintiff has the burden of proving their case by a preponderance of the evidence , which means the plaintiff merely needs to show that the fact in dispute is more likely than not. A "preponderance of the evidence" and "beyond a reasonable doubt" are different standards, requiring different amounts of proof. The burden of proof is often said to consist of two distinct but related concepts: the burden of production , and the burden of persuasion . Depending on the jurisdiction and type of action , the legal standard to satisfy the burden of proof in U.S. litigation may include, but is not limited to: beyond a reasonable doubt in criminal law. clear and convincing evidence to prove fraud in will disputes. preponderance of the evidence in most civil cases. probable cause in the acquisition of a warrant or arrest proceeding. reasonable belief as part of establishing probable cause. reasonable suspicion in cases involving police stop and searches. some credible evidence in cases necessitating immediate intervention, like child protective services disputes. some evidence in cases involving inmate discipline. substantial evidence in many appellate cases. [Last reviewed in November of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords burden of proof evidence; burden of proof EVIDENTIARY BURDENS evidence civil procedure criminal procedure REASONABLE DOUBT preponderance burden of persuasion burden of production Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS trial process/advocacy THE LEGAL PROCESS criminal law criminal procedure evidence legal practice/ethics wex articles wex definitions civil procedure courts and procedure criminal law and procedure legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the requirement for written certification of compliance. The Law Explanation states this makes it difficult for the Disclosing Party to prove the Recipient fulfilled their obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'burden of proof' as 'the standard that a party seeking to prove a fact in court must satisfy to have that fact legally established.' Removing the certification significantly weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to meet this burden, therefore a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 2.4 The Recipient shall establish and maintain adequate security measures (including any reasonable security measures proposed by the Disclosing party from time to time) to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthorised access or use.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.4 The Recipient shall establish and maintain minimal security measures to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthorised access or use.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the obligation to 'minimal' security measures creates a conflict with the reasonable care standard expected in handling confidential information, potentially leading to negligence claims if a breach occurs.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly codified for confidentiality agreements, English negligence law dictates a duty of care to prevent foreseeable harm. By specifying 'minimal' security, the contract potentially breaches this duty if such measures are insufficient to protect confidential information, leading to a reasonably foreseeable breach and damages. A court could find that 'minimal' does not meet the standard of reasonable care expected in the circumstances, especially considering the nature of the information being protected.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Duty of Care Lecture | LawTeacher.net Loading Print Reference this Duty of Care Lecture Share this: Facebook Twitter Reddit LinkedIn WhatsApp Duty of Care What is a ‘Duty of Care’? Duty of care constitutes the first of the three primary elements of tort (duty of care, breach and causation). Whilst there are many situations in which an individual might have acted carelessly, unless they have a duty of care to the person harmed by their carelessness, then no claim will arise. This is a key point - whilst a case (or problem question) might present the clearest existence of breach and causation possible, it will fail if duty of care is not present at the time of the breach. Although the term ‘duty of care’ can seem a little alien at first, it can roughly be thought of a responsibility of an individual to not harm others through carelessness. For example, a driver on the road has a responsibility to other road users to not cause an accident through driving carelessly. In other words: they have a duty of care to other road users. Because of its ability to make or break a given case, duty of care is often thought of as a ‘control mechanism’ within the law - essentially, a way for the courts to make a distinction between cases which are legally significant, and therefore worth pursuing, and those cases which do not merit legal attention. The Development of the Duty of Care The legal basis for finding a duty of care has its roots in Donoghue v Stevenson [1932] AC 562. Although, as will be noted below, there exists a more modern test to establish a duty of care, Donoghue v Stevenson provides the theoretical basis for the duty of care, and thus modern negligence, and so it is necessary to be familiar with the case. Before Donoghue v Stevenson , the concept of duty of care did not exist in any particularly notable form within the English law. This meant that unless a plaintiff had a contract with a defendant, they had no means of bringing a case in negligence. This led to a considerable number of injustices when individuals were injured by clear instances of negligence. An example of this phenomenon can be seen in Winterbottom v Wright (1842) 10 M&W 109. In the case, the defendant (Wright) was contracted by the UK’s Postmaster-General to maintain a horse-drawn mail coach in a safe state. The plaintiff (Winterbottom) was also contracted by the Postmaster-General to drive the coach between destinations but was injured when the coach collapsed due to disrepair. Although it was clear that Wright had acted negligently, the courts held that Winterbottom could not sue Wright, because a contract did not exist between the two. In essence, the contract concept of privity prevented legal action. It does not take a huge amount of insight to see the problems that might have arisen as a result of the above decision. For example, a consumer injured by a bottle mistakenly filled with acid rather than soda would have no legal recourse if somebody else purchased the soda for them. Similarly, since road users do not have contracts with each other, none could be said to owe each other a duty of care, no matter how dangerously they were driving. This is clearly problematic - we want our soda producers to ensure their products are safe, and we want drivers on the road to be careful, and the threat of being sued is a useful deterrent to unsafe behaviour. It is, therefore, fortunate that the courts established the generally applicable concept of duty of care in Donoghuev Stevenson . It should be noted that the concept of a duty of care was not created specifically in Donoghue ; instead, it was restricted to a few, highly specific situations. Thus, statements that the courts ‘invented’ or ‘created’ the duty of care concept in Donoghue are false, and should be avoided. Instead the courts can be thought of as greatly widening the remit of the concept. Case in Focus: Donoghue v Stevenson [1932] AC 562 The case itself involved a relatively simple (and now famous) se
perturbation.justification: The change from 'adequate' to 'minimal' security measures creates a contradiction with the expected standard of care in handling confidential information. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that 'minimal' may not meet the standard of reasonable care, especially given the nature of the information. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'duty of care' as a 'responsibility of an individual to not harm others through carelessness.' Specifying 'minimal' security suggests a lower standard of care that could be deemed careless, particularly if a breach occurs. This contradiction falls under the Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction type, as the changed text sets a requirement ('minimal security') that potentially violates the expected standard of reasonable care.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 Each Recipient shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified the Disclosing Party at all times against all liabilities, costs (including legal costs on an indemnity basis), expenses, damages and losses (including any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party) arising from any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 Each Recipient shall indemnify the Disclosing Party, but only for direct losses, arising from any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the indemnity to 'direct losses' significantly restricts the scope of protection for the disclosing party, potentially leaving them under-compensated for foreseeable damages resulting from a breach, which could be challenged under principles of fairness and full compensation.
perturbation.law_explanation: While English law permits parties to define the scope of indemnity, restricting it solely to 'direct losses' may be deemed unreasonable or unconscionable if it demonstrably fails to adequately compensate the Disclosing Party for foreseeable losses stemming from a breach of confidentiality. Courts often interpret indemnity clauses to provide reasonable protection for the indemnified party, and a limitation that effectively nullifies this protection could be challenged based on fairness and the intent to provide full compensation for damages.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: CFPB Announces Judgment Against Debt Relief Company Morgan Drexen | Practical Law What's on Practical Law? Show less Show more CFPB Announces Judgment Against Debt Relief Company Morgan Drexen Practical Law Legal Update w-001-7624 Â (Approx. 3 pages) Ask a question CFPB Announces Judgment Against Debt Relief Company Morgan Drexen byÂ Practical Law Finance Related Content A federal district court entered a final judgment in favor of the CFPB and against debt relief company Morgan Drexen (Morgan). Morgan is required to pay nearly $173 million in restitution and civil money penalties for improperly charging upfront fees and deceiving consumers. On March 18, 2016, the CFPB announced that a federal district court entered a final judgment in the agency's favor and against debt relief company Morgan Drexen (Morgan). The judgment requires Morgan to pay nearly $173 million in restitution and civil money penalties for improperly charging upfront fees and deceiving customers in violation of the Telemarketing Sales Rule and the Dodd-Frank Act . The judgment marks the end of one of the CFPB's longest running cases, which the agency filed in August 2013. In its complaint, the CFPB had alleged that: Consumers typically sought only debt relief services but were nevertheless signed up for bankruptcy services. Morgan performed little or no bankruptcy work for consumers. Morgan used the bankruptcy services contract as a ruse to disguise impermissible upfront fees for debt relief work. The district court ultimately agreed with the CFPB and found that Morgan had misled consumers by requiring them to enter into contracts for both debt relief and bankruptcy-related services. Specifically, the final judgment requires Morgan to: Refrain from collecting any further fees from its customers. Pay restitution in the amount of $132,882,488 and civil money penalties in the amount of $40 million. The final judgment follows a long procedural history, during which the district court found that Morgan had falsified bankruptcy petitions that it claimed to have prepared for its consumers. The falsification resulted in the court entering a default judgment against Morgan, which led to Morgan filing for bankruptcy and the appointment of an administrator to administer the company's shutdown. Because the company is in bankruptcy, any funds paid towards restitution or the civil monetary penalty will have to result from the bankruptcy process. In addition to this final judgment against Morgan, the district court also entered a final judgment against the president and CEO of the company, Walter Ledda, which prohibited him from operating in the debt relief industry and required him to pay: Restitution in the amount of $500,000. Civil money penalties reduced to $1, due to depleted personal assets. The final judgments against Morgan and Ledda, as well as court documents regarding a subsequent contempt proceeding against Morgan's attorneys, who took over the business and continued the illegal scheme, are available on the CFPB website . End of Document Resource ID w-001-7624 Â©Â 2025Â Thomson Reuters. All rights reserved. perturbation.justification: The original contract provided indemnity against all losses, including direct, indirect, or consequential. The changed text limits this indemnity to only 'direct losses'. The Perturbation Explanation states this significantly restricts the scope of protection, leaving the disclosing party potentially under-compensated. The Law Explanation supports this, stating such a restriction 'may be deemed unreasonable or unconscionable' if it fails to adequately compensate for foreseeable losses. While the scraped snippets do not directly address indemnity clauses, the provided explanations strongly suggest a contradiction because limiting indemnity to direct losses could be viewed as an unreasonable attempt to avoid compensating the Disclosing Party for damages.
